title: Psalms 129
subtitle: They Have Greatly Oppressed Me from My Youth

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Song of Ascent

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “But They Have Not Gained the Victory”

heb.paragraph: "Ploughmen have ploughed (ḥărash) my back and made their furrows long" (v.3). Israel’s communal testimony: greatly oppressed from youth (v.1) but not defeated (v.2). The ploughing image: the back scarred like a ploughed field. Deep wounds, long scars. But: "The LORD is righteous; he has cut me free from the cords of the wicked" (v.4). Freedom through God’s righteousness.

notes.note: "They have greatly oppressed me from my youth — but they have not gained the victory over me." The national testimony: constant oppression, never victory for the oppressors.
"Ploughmen have ploughed my back. But the LORD has cut me free from the cords of the wicked." The wounds are real; the freedom is real. Both are affirmed.

refs.note: Stephen recounts Israel's history of persecution, echoing 'from my youth' oppression.
The faithful endured persecution, torture, and death throughout Israel's history.
Paul's sufferings continue the pattern of affliction that did not prevail.

hist.context: Psalm 129 is a communal lament recounting Israel's long history of oppression. 'They have greatly oppressed me from my youth'—the nation personified speaks. The vivid imagery of plowmen making furrows on the back suggests torture or severe subjugation. Yet the refrain: 'they have not gained the victory over me.' Israel endures.

heb.paragraph: "May all who hate Zion be like grass (ḥāṣîr) on the roof, which withers before it can grow" (v.6). Rooftop grass has no root: thin soil, intense sun, quick death. The enemies of Zion will be like that: no substance, no harvest, no blessing. No reaper fills his hands with it; no one passing by says, "The blessing of the LORD be on you" (vv.7–8). The enemies receive no blessing — not even the casual greeting of a passer-by.

notes.note: "Like grass on the roof, which withers before it can grow. No reaper fills his hands." Rootless growth: impressive briefly, dead soon. No harvest value.
"May those who pass by not say, ‘The blessing of the LORD be on you.’" Even the standard greeting is withheld. The enemies of Zion receive no blessing from anyone.

refs.note: Rootless plants scorched by sun picture those without depth—like grass on rooftops.
The wicked fade like grass; do not fret over evildoers.
All flesh is grass that withers, but the word of our God stands forever.

hist.context: The curse against oppressors invokes rooftop grass—plants that sprout in thin soil on flat roofs but wither before harvest. Harvesters and passers-by ignore them; no blessing is pronounced. The withholding of the harvest blessing ('The blessing of the LORD be on you') is significant: oppressors are cut off from covenant community. The psalm affirms: Israel's afflicters will prove transient.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in They Have Greatly Oppressed Me from My Youth.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in They Have Greatly Oppressed Me from My Youth.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.text: Solomon is not a key figure in Psalm 129

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“They have greatly oppressed me from my youth,” let Israel say;ESV\u201cGreatly have they afflicted me from my youth\u201d\u2014 let Israel now say\u2014NIVBut the Lord is righteous; he has cut me free from the cords of the wicked.”ESVThe LORD is righteous; he has cut the cords of the wicked.

rec.text: ""They have greatly oppressed me from my youth — but they have not gained the victory over me." The national testimony: constant oppression, never victory for the oppressors."

rows.label: Rooftop Grass — 129:5–8
rows.text: Like GRASS ON THE ROOF: no root, no harvest, no blessing. Even passers-by withhold their greeting.

hebtext.note: "Ploughmen have ploughed (ḥărash) my back and made their furrows long" (v.3). Israel’s communal testimony: greatly oppressed from youth (v.1) but not defeated (v.2). The ploughing image: the back scarred like a ploughed field. Deep wounds, long scars. But: "The LORD is righteous; he has cut me free from the cords of the wicked" (v.4). Freedom through God’s righteousness.

"May all who hate Zion be like grass (ḥāṣîr) on the roof, which withers before it can grow" (v.6). Rooftop grass has no root: thin soil, intense sun, quick death. The enemies of Zion will be like that: no substance, no harvest, no blessing. No reaper fills his hands with it; no one passing by says, "The blessing of the LORD be on you" (vv.7–8). The enemies receive no blessing — not even the casual greeting of a passer-by.

themes.note: Justice peaks: the oppressed survive; the oppressors wither. Faith: constant oppression, never ultimate defeat. The ploughed-back image is visceral.
